speaker: interviewer
text: We're on tape-- excuse me. We're on tape seven with Mr. Ervin Chaim Greenfeld, November 15, 1995. Mr. Greenfeld, you were telling me about your embroidery and your cantorial music. And I wanted you to tell me a little bit about your children and your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: My children, my grandchildren-- there's a Hebrew word for-- [HEBREW]. And I don't know how to, how to translate it. I don't think [LAUGHS] there is a translation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pride.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pride, OK. They choose themselves not because of me. Maybe my wife had more influence. They're very devoted and servants in their field. They all choose to be working in the educational field. Devoted is not even enough because-- because they are really-- my son who lives downstairs, he's the most well-liked teacher in [? Babov. ?] Out of 70 teachers, or 50, I don't even know how many teachers, but children are waiting to get into his class because he's very well-liked. My youngest son is in Montreal. He's teaching in the morning and principal in the afternoon. And thank God he's a very clever. A lot of-- very talented boy. Very talented boy. I should have actually started with my daughter, who is a beautiful girl full of heart and exceptional good [NON-ENGLISH]. And a very warm person, very warm person. And she has beautiful three children. Pretty soon we have to look around to find some nice boys for his daughters. She has-- they have two nice daughters and a very fine young man, their son, a real sharp young man, very intelligent boy. He just-- he was [INAUDIBLE] last year, and he made a big, big, big hit with his speech, not because of his speech, but he's a very intelligent boy. He didn't need anybody to write him a speech. My oldest son is a very devoted young man and lives in Muncie. That boy cares for-- about everybody except [LAUGHS] himself. He has poor people in his house. He's involved in family problems, people who have problems in their-- husband and wife problems, they come to him, and he's advising them. And he has an open house where people could come and stay. And they don't-- they don't even know when to leave. [LAUGHS] And he's a very, very full of heart, a man of [NON-ENGLISH]. He's known, he's known in Muncie as one of the-- and Muncie's a town. I don't really know about it. But Muncie is, is, is on the Jewish map as the city of learning. Hundreds and hundreds of young people who choose learning as their goal in life, and he's one of them. He's teaching. He's giving lectures at three or four places for-- one class is for [NON-ENGLISH]. The other one is for Sephardic boys who did not have, did not have a decent family home. You know, the Sephardic people went through a lot of changes. Originally, they were very, very fanatically from a religious-- but they came to Israel, and because of political reasons, they lost their identity. The parents and children were two different world. And now many of them are returning to their roots. And these, these Sephardic people, they didn't have anything like what we call culture. The only thing they had in their-- back in their old country was their very, very devoted religion, but they were not learned enough. And now my son picked up a bunch of them. And he's every year, about 50, 60 are going through his school. And they are all back into a normal-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to just explain that learning means learning Torah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Learning means learning Torah. But they are all pretty well up-to-date. They have a better vocabulary than their father. They all speak better English [LAUGHS] than me. My oldest son is well read. Many, many, many, many books he-- he read through, books which I would probably not even understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, is there any message that you would like to say to your-- either to your children, grandchildren, or just in general about--

speaker: interviewee
text: About the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --what you went through, and maybe what helped you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the survival is something which we have no-- we had no control over it. One thing I know that people who did have faith had a little easier time to-- for survival. And after the war, they didn't give up as easy as many of those who did not have a faith. As far as a general picture, a general statement of mine, I encourage everybody who did survive to put their stories down. No matter how much we're going to talk about it, no matter how much we're going to write about it or read about it or showing it in the movies, will not be the whole story. I was very much influenced in my thinking with the philosophy or thinking of this great Rabbi Weissmandl. And he had left a message. And I just-- in general, unfortunately, the leadership of our people were not in good hands those days. There is a, there is a, there is a passage in the Bible, you should not stand by at your brother's blood. That means that when it's a question of saving a Jewish life, nothing, but nothing can be in the way. If the full story would be written or will be written, a lot, a lot, a lot of mistakes were done by people who didn't understand the, the importance of this passage.

speaker: interviewer
text: They made, they made all type of figures. You're not allowed to send money to the enemy's field. If you are dealing with-- with the people who came to hide-- not hide, but kidnap your son, you're not going to make too much question. You're not going to ask them too many questions. I want my son. Cost? What cost? Whatever. I'm ready to give anything or everything to save his life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And unfortunately, those people who were in charge those days, they were not up to that command of the, of the Bible. They were not ready to understand the-- the-- this was-- this man was who lived it. He gave up his own family and devoted his whole life. And he passed away very early, was a genius, and was a holy man. They called him, even in the secular courts in Israel, he says, that was the holy man, because he gave everything. Nothing-- nothing existed for him except saving people. He was the one who was arguing and bargaining and paying, bribing those monstrous murderers and saved, saved thousands of lives. And if he could be-- he would-- if he would be understood by the secular Jewish leadership to give him enough money, he could've saved another-- more than another million people. This is a-- this is an established fact. Nobody will argue about it. It came out in open, in the open courts. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was-- you mentioned to me that he was [NON-ENGLISH]. So I want you to say that on the tape, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were very close. Even before the war, I heard about him because I had about six uncles who were learning in together with him. I did mention the other, I think in a previous [INAUDIBLE], that he had an uncle who was, who was ran over by a train. He was his closest friend. He was a very special joined. I didn't-- I wasn't close enough to him, but about him, I read about a lot. And I-- I understand what he was to trying to do. Unfortunately, he did not have the means to-- to-- He wasn't, he wasn't understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any message that you want to give other than what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is that it is something which I hope we will never have to experience. But this-- the experience is this, that the inevitable-- the world-- there's a passage [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], we know it that Esau hated Jacob all the time. But we had to have enough-- we were always watched. Somebody was watching over us.

speaker: interviewer
text: The way we looked many, many years ago, 50 years ago, we never expected that we going to survive it and establish-- reestablish. And many, many, many people of these survivors are very successful. Should I mention to you the Reichmann brothers? I'm not talking about money-wise only.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in most cases, it was because the Jewish spirit is something which is there. And we have an Almighty God who is watching over us. We are not smart enough to, to take out, to take out the-- the hate from [NON-ENGLISH] of the next guys. This is just bologna. We are not going to change them. What we have to do is accept this terrible punishment that we got and go further and reestablish and strengthen ourselves in, in, in faith in Almighty God. Because if not for His help, we wouldn't be around.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. The fact is that how many peoples were on the map since creation? Hundreds of nations disappeared. And the only thing which has kept us, even before we had a so-called established political country like Israel, the Jews were around, because there was the Jewish spirit of the Torah, which kept us higher in spirit than the rest of them. And that's why they don't like us. Maybe, maybe [LAUGHS] this will be heard by a non-Jew. He will, he will, he will not like this statement, but these are, these are the facts. No, but these are your feelings, so that's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- I didn't-- I really didn't prepare a statement. This is just while I'm talking, this is how, how it comes out. But I didn't have a special message to-- prepared message. My only message is that children should know about it. The youngsters should know about it because the forgetfulness is a terrible thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was Napoleon who said it many hundreds-- a couple hundred years ago. He was, he was questioned. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. And he saw his soldiers are fasting. He said, what happened? He says, they're fasting about the temple. What happened to the temple? It was burned out. It was destroyed. When did it happen? Oh, it happened about 1,800 years ago. And you are still mourning over it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a people who can mourn of a tragedy which happened 1,700 years ago will never disappear. This was Napoleon. I don't know it word by word, but this is the, this is the point that he wanted to make. So we-- even if we are going to have the whole Middle East governed by, by Israel, still, the Shoah was still a Shoah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we're going to stop there. OK. OK. Thank you for doing the interview, and we're going to do the pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. This is the picture of my grandfather, Zigmund Greenfeld. Or in Jewish, they called him Zelig Greenfeld. He was 84 when they took him to Auschwitz. This picture was about two years before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Gyongyos, in his hometown. I think this is a picture for the passport. This is the picture of my mother just a few days before she got married in 1917. Her name, Gisella Fischer. This is a day or two days before she married-- she was married to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was also in Gyongyos?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that's where they-- that's where the wedding was.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH], Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- that time it was Hungary, but later became Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our family picture. It was taken in [NON-ENGLISH] at the 25th anniversary of my parents. My father, my mother, three brothers, and three sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Start from the front--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Let's go by the right, upper right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sister, Lilli. She was born in 1925. She was a beautiful girl. She was the last one who died. Next is Edith, or Pearl. She was my older sister. She was born in 1920-- in 1917-- 1918. She must've been about 21, 23 years old. The next one is my brother. He's Naftali. He lives in Israel. He's the only brother, the only survivor of my brothers and sisters. This is-- the next one is Ilona, or Yentl. She was my-- she was the second in line. She was the next to Pearl. Now, on the-- in the lower-left is supposed to be me sitting next to my mother. She was beautiful, and the picture is still beautiful. Her name, Gisella Greenfeld, or Gisella Fischer. Next is my father, Nathan Greenfeld. He passed away here in the United States in 1969.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my youngest brother, Udi-- or Abram Udi. He's the one about whom I didn't hear anything since he was taken to Auschwitz. He was supposed to be taken in some kind of a children's camp, children's lager. And somebody read about the children's lager, and I know about this. They were kept there for awhile. And every few weeks, they were selected. And unfortunately, they were thrown into the gas chambers and perished there. This is a picture from my school years. It was one of those Purim plays. I played the clown. That was my role. You can see my picture in the middle with the three buttons on my hat. And on same picture, my sister, Lilli, plays also. She has the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Headband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Headband on her hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: She's the second from the right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second from the right. The rest of the children are from the same Jewish school. And some of them survived. Most of them didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been also in the '38, '39-- 30-- 30-- 38-- around 1938. This is a picture of me about a half a year after the war was over. It's an ID card in [NON-ENGLISH], Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture from the school years when I came out to America. It was on my passport from 1946 [BACKGROUND NOISE] in [NON-ENGLISH]. This is the family picture of my wife's family, who survived miraculously. My mother-in-law and five small children in Viennese-- in-- in-- in Vienna, they were in a family lager. And my father-in-law was in the same places where I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me when this was taken. Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture must've been taken when they came up to the, to the United States, which was 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still taken in Europe, just before they got the visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yisroel Brown, this is my father-in-law. And his wife, Anna, and the five children. My wife is in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the bottom or on top?

speaker: interviewee
text: On top. Ruchel is on the right side. Chaim is on the left side. Moishe on the lower part, and Nichu, [BACKGROUND NOISE] the youngest, is in the middle. She was about two years, three years old. This is our wedding picture from 1952, my wife and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife's first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Irene [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in the Grand Paradise in Williamsburg, Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brooklyn?

speaker: interviewee
text: You wouldn't go there today.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. But Brooklyn, New York, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brooklyn, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is from my oldest grandson's bar mitzvah. Let's go from the right. It's my oldest granddaughter, [? Parella ?], then her father, Michael. This is the bar mitzvah boy in the middle. And sitting--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name? His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Anusy ?]. Then sitting is [? Rohella ?], the second daughter of my daughter. Now comes my daughter in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Giti. Giti is my daughter. Next to her is me. In front of me to my right is my wife, Rifka. But let's not forget about my lovely great-- great-grandmother of this bar mitzvah [NON-ENGLISH]. They call her Sara [NON-ENGLISH], or [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a picture of my lovely stepmother, Sara [NON-ENGLISH], and myself. It was taken by my grandson's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which was about a half a year ago in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same bar mitzvah, some of my grandchildren are celebrating with their great-grandmother, my daughter and some friends. This is a picture of my brother, Naftali, and his bride, Eliza, from way back from 1946, almost 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this-- where was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: I just was going to say that he's the only surviving brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] in Slovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Slovakia, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This book is called based on the life of this great man, Rabbi Michael Dov Weissmandl, who was besides being the famous [NON-ENGLISH], a giant of the Holocaust [BACKGROUND NOISE] as the greatest hero of that period. This picture was just actually a paint, but the book is about his life story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Greenfeld, please introduce this lovely lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Rifka. She is my wife for the past 43 and 1/2 years, and we have plenty more to go, [NON-ENGLISH], with God's help. Now you say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea about what was going on here till now. But the only thing I would like to say is that since Germany was the most cultured country at the time of the Holocaust, and that culture led to the slaughter of 6 million of our people, I would definitely like to tell my children and anyone else who's interested that they should stay with our culture, which spreads love and understanding and help, instead of trying to alienate alien cultures, which led to the most brutal slaughter of our people in the, in the whole--

speaker: interviewee
text: History.

speaker: interviewee
text: --history of humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for doing the tape. Thank.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
